questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with hypertension presents with sudden, painless, blurred vision in the right eye noticed on waking. Visual acuity is 6/9. Fundoscopy shows flame-shaped hemorrhages in the superotemporal quadrant of the retina. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The superotemporal quadrant is the most common site for BRVO due to arteriovenous crossings. The absence of pain and presence of flame-shaped haemorrhages in a quadrant point to BRVO.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with diabetes and hypertension presents with sudden, painless loss of central vision in her left eye over 2 days. Visual acuity is hand movements. Fundoscopy reveals a swollen optic disc, venous tortuosity, and diffuse retinal haemorrhages in all four quadrants. There is a relative afferent pupillary defect. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of RAPD, severe vision loss, and haemorrhages in all quadrants is classic for ischaemic CRVO. AION typically shows altitudinal field defect and no retinal haemorrhages; CRAO shows cherry-red spot; optic neuritis is painful with eye movement; GCA would have systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known central retinal vein occlusion in his right eye three months ago now presents with a red, painful right eye. Visual acuity is perception of light. Intraocular pressure is 42 mmHg. Gonioscopy shows new vessels on the iris and angle. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Neovascular glaucoma is a known complication of ischaemic CRVO, presenting with rubeosis iridis, high IOP, and pain. The history of CRVO and new vessels is diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman on the oral contraceptive pill presents with mild, painless blurred vision in her left eye for 3 days. Visual acuity is 6/9. Fundoscopy shows optic disc oedema, venous engorgement, and some flame-shaped haemorrhages around the disc. No RAPD. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Papillophlebitis is a type of CRVO typically occurring in young women, often associated with OCP use. It presents with disc oedema and venous engorgement without severe vision loss or RAPD, unlike optic neuritis.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with hypertension presents with sudden, painless central vision loss. Fundoscopy shows a swollen optic disc, very tortuous veins, and a 'blood and thunder' appearance with diffuse haemorrhages. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: 'Blood and thunder' fundus is pathognomonic for central retinal vein occlusion. The other options lack this extensive haemorrhagic pattern.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old male presents with sudden painless loss of vision in the right eye over 24 hours. Visual acuity is 6/60. Fundoscopy reveals extensive retinal hemorrhages in all quadrants, cotton wool spots, and dilated tortuous retinal veins. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fluorescein angiography is the gold standard for confirming retinal vein occlusion by showing delayed filling and areas of capillary non-perfusion. Optical coherence tomography is useful for assessing macular edema but does not confirm the occlusion. The other options are less specific.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old female with known central retinal vein occlusion undergoes fluorescein angiography. Which of the following findings on fluorescein angiography is most consistent with an ischemic CRVO?
questions.explanation: Ischemic CRVO is defined by ≥10 disc areas of capillary non-perfusion on fluorescein angiography. Lesser areas suggest non-ischemic CRVO. Cystoid macular edema and leakage can occur in both types.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male with hypertension reports blurring in the inferonasal field of his left eye. Fundoscopy shows a few flame-shaped hemorrhages in the superior temporal quadrant, consistent with a branch retinal vein occlusion. The macula appears clinically normal. What is the single most appropriate investigation to assess for macular involvement?
questions.explanation: Optical coherence tomography is highly sensitive for detecting subtle macular edema and is the preferred first-line investigation to assess macular involvement in BRVO. Fluorescein angiography can also show edema but is more invasive and less sensitive for small amounts of fluid.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old male with a history of central retinal vein occlusion presents with a red, painful right eye and cloudy cornea. Intraocular pressure is 48 mmHg. Slit lamp examination shows corneal edema. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of neovascular glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Neovascular glaucoma is diagnosed by identifying neovascularization of the iris and angle. Gonioscopy is the gold standard to visualize angle neovascularization. While other tests may show secondary changes, they do not confirm the presence of angle neovascularization.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old female presents with acute painless vision loss in the left eye. Fundoscopy shows a central retinal vein occlusion. She has no history of hypertension, diabetes, or glaucoma. What is the single most appropriate investigation to identify a potential underlying prothrombotic state?
questions.explanation: In young patients (<50 years) with CRVO, routine thrombophilia screening is recommended, with antiphospholipid syndrome being a common and treatable cause. Full blood count and plasma viscosity are basic but less specific for prothrombotic state. Homocysteine and Factor V Leiden are also relevant but not the single best initial screen.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with previously good vision presents with a 2-week history of blurred vision in the right eye. On fundoscopy, he has a CRVO with macular oedema confirmed on OCT (central subfield thickness 532 μm). There is no evidence of neovascularisation. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Anti-VEGF therapy (ranibizumab or aflibercept) is first-line for centre-involving macular oedema secondary to CRVO. Dexamethasone implant is second-line or if anti-VEGF unsuitable. Laser is not effective for CRVO macular oedema.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with blurred vision in the left eye due to a BRVO. OCT shows macular oedema but the fovea is spared (central subfield thickness 280 μm) and her visual acuity is 6/9. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For BRVO with non-centre-involving macular oedema, grid laser is effective and first-line. Anti-VEGF is reserved for centre-involving oedema. Observation may be considered if asymptomatic, but here she is symptomatic.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with CRVO and macular oedema is noted to have poorly controlled open-angle glaucoma (intraocular pressure 28 mmHg on three topical agents). Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Dexamethasone implant can cause a significant rise in intraocular pressure and is relatively contraindicated in poorly controlled glaucoma. Anti-VEGF agents and laser do not have this effect.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a known CRVO presents with acute, painful red eye, and vision reduced to hand motions. Intraocular pressure is 45 mmHg and gonioscopy reveals neovascularisation of the angle. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents cause rapid regression of iris and angle neovascularization, lowering IOP, and are first-line in acute neovascular glaucoma. PRP is required later but anti-VEGF is the initial priority.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with BRVO and centre-involving macular oedema has had three monthly injections of ranibizumab with poor anatomical response (persistent oedema). She has a history of recurrent herpes simplex keratitis. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids can reactivate herpes simplex keratitis, so dexamethasone implant is contraindicated. Other options are safe second-line choices.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of painless blurred vision in the left eye. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Visual acuity is 6/9. Fundoscopy reveals a superotemporal branch retinal vein occlusion with intraretinal haemorrhages. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check blood pressure and arrange GP follow-up
questions.explanation: The immediate next step after diagnosing a branch retinal vein occlusion in the emergency department is to refer urgently to ophthalmology for assessment and treatment planning. Fluorescein angiography and anti-VEGF therapy are specialist interventions performed by ophthalmology. Aspirin has no role, and while blood pressure control is important, referral takes priority.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO) in the right eye presents to the eye casualty with severe eye pain, redness, and nausea. On examination, her right eye is erythematous, cornea is oedematous, and intraocular pressure is 42 mmHg. Gonioscopy shows neovascularisation of the iris and angle. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to glaucoma specialist for trabeculectomy
questions.explanation: This is neovascular glaucoma secondary to CRVO. The immediate priority is to lower the intraocular pressure rapidly. Intravenous acetazolamide is the fastest method. Topical pilocarpine is ineffective in neovascular glaucoma. Panretinal photocoagulation is definitive but not immediate. Surgery is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of CRVO in the left eye on follow-up presents with sudden onset of floaters and a shadow in the peripheral vision of the left eye. He denies pain. Visual acuity is 6/12 (previously 6/9). Fundoscopy reveals a new vitreous haemorrhage but no obvious retinal tear. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise bedrest and follow up in 2 weeks
questions.explanation: The patient likely has a vitreous haemorrhage from new vessels secondary to CRVO. Urgent referral to ophthalmology is needed for assessment and to rule out retinal tear or detachment, and for consideration of panretinal photocoagulation. Ultrasound is part of the ophthalmology assessment. Anti-VEGF is not immediate. Tranexamic acid is not used.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with normotension and no cardiovascular risk factors presents to her GP with a 1-week history of blurred vision in the right eye. On fundoscopy, she has a superotemporal BRVO with mild macular oedema. She is otherwise well. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check erythrocyte sedimentation rate and CRP
questions.explanation: In a patient with BRVO without obvious risk factors, an underlying inflammatory or hypercoagulable state should be considered. Checking ESR and CRP is the immediate step to screen for giant cell arteritis or vasculitis. Referral to ophthalmology is important but not the immediate next step in primary care; the GP should investigate causes first. Aspirin is not indicated. OCT is done by ophthalmology.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with known hypertension and diabetes presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of vision loss in the left eye. Fundoscopy reveals a central retinal vein occlusion with extensive haemorrhages. He is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation. His INR is 3.5. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Stop warfarin and give vitamin K
Reduce warfarin dose to target INR 2-3
questions.explanation: The immediate next step is to refer to ophthalmology urgently for assessment and management of the CRVO. Managing the warfarin is important but should be done in consultation with ophthalmology, as there is no evidence that anticoagulation causes CRVO or alters management; stopping or reversing warfarin may increase thrombotic risk. Anti-VEGF and angiography are ophthalmology decisions.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with sudden, painless, blurred vision in the right eye. Fundoscopy reveals disc oedema, tortuous dilated veins, and diffuse intraretinal haemorrhages in all quadrants. Which single finding on examination would indicate that this is an ischaemic CRVO requiring urgent ophthalmology assessment?
questions.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) indicates significant retinal ischaemia in central retinal vein occlusion, which carries a high risk of neovascularisation and requires urgent ophthalmology review. Macular oedema is common but not a marker of ischaemia; iris neovascularisation is a later complication.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman presents with sudden visual loss in her left eye. Fundoscopy shows a cherry-red spot at the fovea and marked retinal opacification. Which condition must be urgently excluded?
questions.explanation: A cherry-red spot with retinal whitening is pathognomonic for central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), an ocular emergency often due to emboli. It must be distinguished from CRVO, which typically shows diffuse haemorrhages without a cherry-red spot.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with a history of IV drug use presents with fever and blurred vision. Fundoscopy reveals multiple retinal haemorrhages with pale centres. Which finding should raise suspicion for an underlying systemic infection?
questions.explanation: Roth spots are retinal haemorrhages with a pale centre, strongly associated with infective endocarditis and other vasculitides. They should prompt urgent investigation for sepsis, distinguishing them from cotton wool spots (ischaemic) or hard exudates (chronic).

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with hypertension presents with acute, painful red eye and decreased vision. He has a history of central retinal vein occlusion in the same eye 4 months ago. On slit-lamp examination, which finding indicates neovascular glaucoma?
options: Flare and cells in anterior chamber
questions.explanation: Iris neovascularisation (rubeosis iridis) is the precursor to neovascular glaucoma in CRVO, indicating severe retinal ischaemia. It is a red flag requiring urgent laser treatment. Flare and cells suggest uveitis; shallow anterior chamber suggests angle closure.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with antiphospholipid syndrome presents with sudden vision loss in the right eye. Fundoscopy shows a branch retinal vein occlusion with retinal haemorrhages in the inferotemporal quadrant. Which examination finding would suggest that this is not a simple BRVO but rather an ocular ischaemic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Ocular ischaemic syndrome due to carotid artery stenosis can mimic BRVO but typically presents with iris neovascularisation, low IOP, and retinal findings. The presence of iris neovascularisation in a young patient with hypercoagulable state should prompt urgent carotid evaluation.
